title: What is Fine-Grained Classification?
concept: Fine-grained classification aims to distinguish between visually similar sub-categories within a common parent category.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main difference between single-label and multi-label classification?

options.text: Single-label outputs one mutually exclusive class; multi-label outputs multiple independent labels.
Single-label uses Sigmoid; multi-label uses Softmax.
Multi-label classification does not use loss functions.

exercises.explain: Correct! Single-label assumes one category per image, whereas multi-label allows multiple tags independently.

cards.title: Siberian Husky vs. Alaskan Malamute
cards.body: To a general image classifier, they both look like 'dogs'. But to a dog breeder, they are completely different breeds with distinct characteristics. Distinguishing these extremely similar breeds is the goal of fine-grained classification.

cards.title: What is Fine-Grained?
cards.body: Fine-grained classification is a task where a model must distinguish between highly similar sub-categories within a single overarching category (such as specific bird species, car makes and models, or flower types).

cards.title: A generalist vs. a specialist
cards.body: Think of general classification as a general practitioner who can tell a bird from a plane. Fine-grained classification is like a specialist ornithologist who can tell a Caspian Tern from an Elegant Tern by looking at the beak's exact curve.

cards.title: Car identification
cards.body: A general classifier labels an image as 'Car'. A fine-grained classifier identifies it specifically as a '2018 Tesla Model 3 Long Range', recognizing subtle features like headlights, grill patterns, and body shape.

cards.title: Not just more classes
cards.body: You might think fine-grained classification is just standard classification with a larger number of categories. In reality, it is much harder because the visual differences between classes are tiny, while the differences within a single class (due to pose/lighting) can be huge.

left.text:
Classes: [Cat, Dog, Bird]
High visual differences
Easier for standard CNN backbones

right.text:
Classes: [Bald Eagle, Golden Eagle]
Extremely low visual differences
Requires local detail attention

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a fine-grained classification task?
exercises.explain: Identifying specific species of a single animal family is a classic fine-grained classification problem.

options.text: Distinguishing between 200 different species of birds
Separating images of birds from images of fish
Detecting where a bird is located inside an image
Counting the total number of birds in a tree

exercises.prompt: True or false: Fine-grained classification is generally easier than coarse-grained classification because the images belong to the same domain.
exercises.explain: False. It is much harder because the visual differences between distinct sub-categories are extremely subtle and easy for models to miss.
Standard CNNs focus on global shape, but fine-grained classification needs local details like eye color or texture patterns.
exercises.sentence: Fine-grained classification requires the model to focus on minute, ___ visual details.

exercises.prompt: Which prediction label represents a fine-grained classification output?
exercises.explain: Specifying the exact make, model, and trim year of a vehicle requires fine-grained classification.

exercises.prompt: You are designing an app for botanists to classify mushrooms in the forest. Many poisonous mushrooms look identical to edible ones except for the texture under the cap. Why will a standard ResNet trained on raw images likely fail?

options.text: Because standard models focus on global shapes (like 'cap and stem') rather than local cap-underbelly textures.
Because mushrooms are too small to be processed by convolutional networks.
Because ResNets are only designed for 3-channel RGB images, not mushrooms.
Because botanists use different mathematical systems than deep learning.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Standard CNNs look at overall structures first. In fine-grained tasks, the overall structures are identical, so the model must learn to zoom in and focus on local features. Next, let's explore this core challenge of visual variance!
